How to train your first RL Language Model using Prime Lab (Full Guide)
This is not a sponsored post. I just like companies that sell the things exactly as they advertise on their landing page. So this is a guide for you to get started with training your own language models with Prime Labs.
This article will also help you appreciate just how much heavy lifting is needed to get models trained and deployed to prod.
Pre-requisites:
  • A Prime Intellect account
  • Access to terminal
  • Internet!
Note to readers: If you spot an error, please let me know - I'll update this guide swiftly!
K lets begin!

1. Setup

bash
# install uv
curl -LsSf https://astral.sh/uv/install.sh | sh 

# install the prime CLI 
uv tool install -U prime

# Login
prime login
This will open Prime intellect on your browser, copy the auth token and paste it in your terminal, and you are IN! You can start interacting with prime's infrastructure already.

2. Setting up agent lab

bash
# Run this command
prime lab setup
The above command is simple, but prime is doing a couple of things when you run this command:
One, it add skills with AGENTS .md Prime has a ton of skills designed to help your coding agent (claude, codex, cursor, opencode, etc) to help you with your development. This contains information on how to create your own RL environments, find already created environments, train, brainstorm, and more.
These skills are not for the agents only, you can read these skill. md files yourself - they contain a wealth of knowledge about how to optimize and design good RL environments. They contain info about how to use prime rl primitives to design RL environments as well.
Second thing: install verifiers. Verifiers is a library for creating environments to train and evaluate LLMs.
This library is the main driver for most of the things that happen under the hood.
Before we proceed to the next stage in this guide, let's step back and understand what constitutes a typical training run.
Prime Labs and Hosted Training abstracts away all this complexity from you with a simple config-driven training. But to really appreciate this framework, we need to go a bit deeper to understand each layer of this stack.
Let's first understand the foundations for an end-to-end AI Research Stack.

3. What does it take to be an AI Lab?

Let's take a beat and understand what it takes to train and deploy AI.

3.1 : Tasks and Datasets

First of all, you need environments. Think of an environment as a simulation or a data-engine where your agent takes actions, observes how it's actions change the world, and learn from it's experiences.
- A dataset of prompts and scenarios: Agent observes these to take actions. A dataset can be a literal CSV full of prompts, and also procedurally generated scenarios (like a wordle generator for example). - A harness of tools: Example: You want to make a coding agent. You provide the agent a sandbox to execute it's Python code in. Tools allow agents to take actions and the environment to verify if the actions worked. - A rubric for rewards: Inform the agent if their action has led to positive rewards or not. In RL we use this reward signal to train the agent to learn from its experience and take better actions in the future.
The verifiers library provides you a way to design your own environments, provide specific sandboxed tools (like code execution), and write reward functions.
Prime Intellect's Environment Hub is a collaborative and largely community-driven github-like for text-based environments.
Environments are not just for LLM finetuning but they can also be thought of as evals. These environments let you test how good your models are in these specific cool tasks, and also train them with RL.
will brown
@willccbb
·
Feb 3
Replying to
@ErdalToprak
evals = environments

3.2: Intelligence and Algorithms

The environment design is SEPERATE from the model training algorithm.
The environment provides the rules about the task you want to train, and feedback on how the agent is doing. The intelligence layer implements HOW training is done. About the agentic loop where the agent observes -> acts -> receives rewards -> trains -> wins.
Feast your eyes on this RL Slide I had lying around in my computer!
Introducing the PRIME-RL library.
Prime-RL is a large-scale model training library that integrates with verifiers and their infra platform. It contains implementations for RL algorithms with Parameter Efficient LORA finetuning!
Interacting with it is pretty intuitive, you'll see in Section 5!


3.3 : Infrastructure

We talked about environments and intelligence, but LLMs require hardware to train.
You need GPUs, pods, and infra management. They provide instances for rent to train your models on. Storages, spot instances, clusters, etc too.
The cool thing?

With Hosted Training, you don't actually need to configure the instances yourself.

Of course, you can rent your own instance and run a docker container to go bananas with a custom training loop.
Hosted Training via Lab is fully managed prime-rl, no GPU configuration needed whatsoever, and uses a new config/entrypoint.

4. Back to tutorial

Sorry for the long detour. I just wanted yall to understand what a "AI stack" looks like. You need datasets, environments/evals, rubrics, models, training algorithms, gpus, clusters, storage. Prime provides a way for all of this to be accessible to end-users.
Once you run prime lab setup it installs verifiers (for environments & evals) that integrates you with prime-rl (training algorithms) and their infra hub (for cloud GPUs).
You do not need to deep-dive into all of these components we mentioned (training, verifiers, and prime-rl). You can just define ONE .toml file and get your training up and running.
The steps are simple:
  • You define a config that contains which model you are training, which environment, what hyperparameters, etc.
  • Prime automatically manages the hardware for you - they have presets for every model, so you dont need to configure instances at all!

5. Let's see some use-cases next.

Q. I don't understand what to do at all
In general, I'd suggest to spend time understanding the basics. You will never regret it. If you're just exploring, use an LLM coding agent (cursor/claude/codex etc) and start asking questions. Prime provides Agents .md files to help you brainstorm and learn.
Some of the agents documentation is still old, but hopefully they will be updated in due time.
Run `prime -h` to get all the commands available to you directly from TUI.
You can ask for help like this with any command prefix!
Like testing the `prime env -h` shows terminal commands for manageing verifiers environments. `prime rl -h` helps with RL tasks!

Q. I want to evaluate my model on an existing environment in the Hub
bash
prime eval run wordle -m qwen/qwen3-coder -n 5
Runs your model on the wordle task. Produces 5 synthetic examples. After this smoketest passes, increase n to run a proper benchmark on your base model.
You will see a cool TUI launch that show how the eval is running! You can track the reward your model is getting. Pay attention to both the number of correct answers, partial answers and format_reward.
This works with any OpenAI chat sdk model. You can check out the `configs/endpoints.toml` file for instructions on how to add your own models and API keys to test.

Q. Should I do RFT or SFT?
Running `prime eval tui` launches a TUI that shows your past eval runs!
This is awesome to quickly benchmark models on environments you care about the most
If format_reward is low, your model sucks at following basic instructions and you should first finetune with SFT so it knows how to answer questions in expected formats. Only if your total reward is atleast 20%, you have hopes to jump straight into RL.
In general, if a model is outputting diverse rewards (i.e. atleast 20-40% correct answers on a task), it is very likely you can increase its performance with RFT (Reinforcement Fine Tuning). If its lower, you should use SFT (Supervised Fine Tuning). Generate a dataset with a stronger LLM and train your small model to mimic it.
Checking your base model's eval score is therefore super important as a starting step.
Q. I have a use-case in mind but I dont know which environment is right for me You can search online on their Hub for compatible environments, but also directly from the terminal.
bash
prime env list --search "math"

# View your own environments
prime env list --mine

# Get info
prime env info owner/env-name

# Sort by stars
prime env list --sort stars
Your coding agent also has access to prime skills to search and read descriptions for you - so use it!

Q. I want to create a custom environment for my own task!
This requires a separate deep dive. I'll skip that in this article. But you can read more here:
https://docs.primeintellect.ai/verifiers/environments#your-first-environment
I'll suggest you don't train your first model on a custom environment. First, just use one of the existing ones to learn the playbook.
Q. I want to train a model on an environment
Step 1: First find available models, run: prime rl models
Or you can read this article for available models, their pricing, and guidance:
https://docs.primeintellect.ai/hosted-training/models-and-pricing
In general, prototype fast with a small model (Llama-1B) before moving on to bigger models.
Step 2: Create a training config .toml file with key hyperparameters:
yaml
model = "Qwen/Qwen3-30B-A3B-Instruct-2507"   # HuggingFace model ID

learning_rate = 1e-4                         # Learning rate for LoRA
lora_alpha = 16                              # LoRA alpha scaling factor


[[env]]
id = "me/my-env"  # Use Hub slug after publishing
num_examples = 1000

[training]
batch_size = 128
learning_rate = 1e-6
See all available config settings here:
https://docs.primeintellect.ai/hosted-training/advanced-configs
Example configs are shared in the `configs/rl` directory after you run prime lab setup. You can create a new template config running `prime rl init` and edit it.
Step 3: To train on a config file, use:
bash
# This gives you a run-id
prime rl run path-to-config.toml
You can monitor the training run!

Q. How do I monitor the training run?
You can monitor by running:
`prime rl logs <run-id> -f`
You can track the running experiment with `prime rl list`
You can even check intermediate rollouts of an ongoing run to see what kind of outputs your LLM is generating!
Lastly, if you feel your credits are going away for nothing, just run
`prime rl stop <run_id>` to stop a training run completely.
Q. I dont want to use their compute, I wanna run things on my own hosted-server/locally on my computer
The prime-rl library allows that too.
bash
# Setup prime-rl
prime lab setup --prime-rl

# Start training
uv run prime-rl configs/prime-rl/config.toml

Q. I dont wanna do training, I want to run prompt optimization
GEPA is a popular prompt optimization method.
prime gepa run wiki-search --model google/gemini-3-flash-preview
This will optimize the system prompt for the wiki-search environment using the specified model for both evaluation rollouts and reflection.
Q. I want to deploy a model for inference so others can use it publicly
Once a training run finishes, the LORA adapter is immediately available for deployment!
Run `prime deployment list` to check candidate deployments!
Reading the docs, I am not sure Prime Intellect supports deploying a full base model. Look into other vllm based hosting services for this.
They do however, let you deploy trained LORA adapters easily.
# View your LORAs (appears after training)
prime deployments list

# Deploy a LORA
prime deployments create <adapter_id>

# Call it using OpenAI SDKs with correct base urls
curl -X POST https://api.pinference.ai/api/v1/chat/completions \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $PRIME_API_KEY" \
  -d '{
    "model": <model_name>,
    "messages": [{"role": "user", "content": "Hello"}],
    "max_tokens": 100
  }'
BAM! It's deployed!
You can call it easily! All you need is a API Key (get it from the website)
Run `prime deployments delete <adapter_id>` to delete an existing deployment!

Q. I dont think RL is required for me right now...
According to their latest blogpost
, native support for distillation, SFT is coming soon.
Check the Prime RL official guide:
https://docs.primeintellect.ai/hosted-training/end-to-end-run
